text.title: Error Recovery — Good and Bad Message Examples
text.summary: Real error message examples from Stripe, GitHub, and form validation, plus anti-patterns with failure explanations.

example.note: Stripe payment error: "Your card was declined. Your card number is incorrect. Please check the number and try again." [Try a different card] — all three components present, plain language, specific cause, recovery action provided.

GitHub 404: "This is not the web page you are looking for. The page you requested doesn't exist. You may have typed the address incorrectly or the page may have moved." [Go to GitHub.com] [Search GitHub] — human tone, two recovery paths offered.

Form password validation: "Password must contain: checkmark At least 8 characters / X One uppercase letter (add one) / X One number (add one)" — live checklist format shows both what is satisfied and what remains; users can see progress without re-reading requirements.

antipattern.description: Showing only a technical error code: "Error: ECONNREFUSED" or "Error 403".
antipattern.reason: Technical codes are meaningless to users. They cannot diagnose the problem or take any corrective action. Even "Connection refused" is better than the raw code.
Tells users nothing about what failed, why, or what to do. Generates immediate support escalation because users have no information to self-serve.

antipattern.description: Blame framing: "You entered invalid data" or "You did not complete the form correctly."
antipattern.reason: Accusatory language increases frustration without adding information. Replace with passive or system-focused framing: "This field requires a valid date" rather than "You entered an invalid date."

antipattern.description: Dead-end error with only an [OK] button and no recovery path.
antipattern.reason: Users who dismiss an [OK] dialog are returned to an unchanged state with no guidance. They will either repeat the same action (same failure) or abandon. Always offer at least one forward path.

rule.statement: Do not communicate errors by color change alone — always include an icon or text label alongside color.
rule.rationale: Color-only error indication fails for users with color vision deficiency. Red borders without an error icon or text are invisible to approximately 8% of male users.

rule.statement: Use ARIA live regions or role="alert" to announce errors to screen reader users; move focus to the error message or the first invalid field on form submission.
rule.rationale: Screen reader users do not perceive visual error indicators. Without ARIA announcements and focus management, they may be unaware an error occurred at all.
